none: Chattanooga, Cromwell Road low-rent housing : environmental impact statement.

The Chattanooga Housing Authority plans to build 200 low-rent housing units on Cromwell Road in Chattanooga, Tennessee. This project aims to provide replacement housing for families displaced by the Orchard Knob Urban Renewal Project. The development will be on a 90-acre site currently used by an archery club. While the project addresses a critical housing need, it faces challenges such as noise from nearby transportation and industrial activities, air quality issues due to quarry operations, and the lack of existing water, sewer, and public transportation facilities. The community has expressed typical concerns during public hearings.

The Chattanooga Housing Authority plans to build 200 low-rent housing units on Cromwell Road in Chattanooga, Tennessee. This project aims to provide replacement housing for families displaced by the Orchard Knob Urban Renewal Project. The development will be on a 90-acre site currently used by an archery club. While the project addresses a critical housing need, it faces challenges such as noise from nearby transportation and industrial activities, air quality issues due to quarry operations, and the lack of existing water, sewer, and public transportation facilities. The community has expressed typical concerns during public hearings.
U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development

The Chattanooga Housing Authority proposed this project to address the urgent need for low-rent housing, particularly for families displaced by urban renewal efforts. While the initiative aims to provide essential housing, it has faced community concerns regarding environmental impacts, including noise and air quality issues, as well as infrastructure deficiencies. Public hearings revealed mixed feelings among residents, reflecting the complexities of urban development in the area.
United States. Department of Housing and Urban Development
United States. Department of Housing and Urban Development

"The high readings are caused primarily by the operation of a large quarrying operation in the immediate vicinity as well as limited contributions from large areas of barren soil within 300 meters of the monitoring site." — James M. Lents, Ph.D., Manager, Technical Division, Air Pollution Control Bureau

"The president of the club has indicated to the Housing Authority that they are making attempts to locate another area for continued club use. However, no location has been found at this time." — Tennessee Wildlife Resources Agency
